user_query: I want a small, scheduled job that gives me a calm, non-political start to my day without needing to look at news. Please set up a daily task that fetches NASA’s official "Astronomy Picture of the Day" page (the NASA APOD page hosted under the nasa.gov domain; the page title contains "Astronomy Picture of the Day") and saves both the raw page and a structured summary locally. Requirements: - Scheduling and test run - Create an executable runner that can be called locally (you choose the implementation) and produces the outputs below in one run. - Provide schedule configurations (do not install them, just create the files) so I can enable them later: - output/schedule/cron_entry.txt: one crontab line that runs the runner daily at 07:30 local time and appends stdout/stderr to output/logs/cron.log. - output/schedule/windows_task.xml: a Windows Task Scheduler XML defining an equivalent daily 07:30 run pointing to the same local runner. - Also execute a one-time run now so today’s artifacts exist and I can verify the setup. - Internet fetch and extraction - Resolve and download the official NASA APOD page (identify it by its title and nasa.gov domain; do not hardcode a third-party mirror). Follow redirects. - Save the final fetched URL you used. - Extract from the downloaded HTML: - page_title: the text. - image_urls: all absolute URLs from <img> tags on the page (deduplicate; convert relative to absolute as needed). - caption_snippet: up to the first 200 characters of any visible caption/description text near the main content (best-effort; null if not found). - Non-political filter (I’m averse to political talk) - If page_title or caption_snippet contains any of these substrings (case-insensitive): ["politic", "election", "congress", "senate", "president", "policy", "vote"], treat the run as skipped for content. In that case, set status to "skipped_political" and write image_urls as an empty list, but still save the raw HTML and JSON summary. - Error handling via command output/diagnostics - Classify the outcome by inspecting the web client’s exit status and error output (or equivalent exception info) and record a status field: one of "ok", "http_error", "network_error", "parse_error", "skipped_political". - Also record an error_type when not "ok" (one of: "dns_error", "timeout", "http_status_not_ok", "parse_failure"). Include a brief message extracted from stderr/exception text. - Outputs (all paths are relative to the workspace and must be created): - Raw HTML: output/raw/apod-YYYY-MM-DD.html - Structured JSON summary: output/summaries/apod-YYYY-MM-DD.json with fields exactly: - date (YYYY-MM-DD, local date of run) - source_name (string; use "NASA APOD") - source_page (string; final URL fetched) - page_title (string or null) - status ("ok" | "http_error" | "network_error" | "parse_error" | "skipped_political") - http_status (integer or null) - image_urls (array of strings) - caption_snippet (string or null) - error_type (string or null) - error_message (string or null) - Run log: append one TSV line per run to output/logs/run.log with columns (in this order): timestamp_iso, status, http_status, exit_code, error_type, message, html_path, json_path. - Last run pointer: output/last_run.json with fields: timestamp_iso, status, html_path, json_path, log_path. - Notes - Use only the official NASA APOD page under nasa.gov as the source; do not rely on any unofficial mirrors. - The extraction can be heuristic but must be deterministic for a given page. The Internet step is essential—do not replace it with a local substitute. - Make sure the runner returns a non-zero exit code if status is not "ok" or "skipped_political". Deliverables are the created files under output/ and the runnable scheduled job artifacts. I should be able to re-run the runner manually to regenerate today’s files and see new entries in output/logs/run.log. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
